does: This paper develops HumanAgencyBench, a scalable evaluation framework for measuring how well AI assistants support human agency across six dimensions: asking clarifying questions, avoiding value manipulation, correcting misinformation, deferring important decisions, encouraging learning, and maintaining social boundaries. The authors integrate philosophical and scientific theories of agency with LLM-based evaluation methods, using large language models to generate realistic user queries and systematically evaluate AI assistant responses. The benchmark is applied to contemporary LLM-based assistants, revealing that current systems provide low-to-moderate support for human agency and show substantial variation across different developers and across the six measured dimensions.
connects: This work responds to growing concerns about AI systems steering human behavior, building on decades of research in human-computer interaction, philosophy of agency, and algorithmic accountability. It extends prior work on AI evaluation frameworks and benchmarks by introducing agency—a multifaceted concept grounded in both humanistic philosophy and empirical social science—as a central evaluation criterion. The paper bridges concerns from technology ethics literature about filter bubbles and engagement-driven algorithms with formal AI evaluation methodology, drawing on recent advances in using LLMs as evaluation tools to scale qualitative assessment beyond human raters. It sits at the intersection of AI safety, value alignment, and human-centered AI design.
enables: This benchmark enables systematic measurement and improvement of how AI systems interact with human autonomy, creating accountability mechanisms for developers to optimize agency support alongside traditional metrics. The six-dimensional framework provides a reusable evaluation infrastructure that could guide the design of more respectful AI assistants and inform policy discussions around AI governance and user protection. Future work could expand the benchmark to cover additional interaction contexts, develop techniques to improve agency support in specific dimensions, and investigate causal relationships between agency-supporting behaviors and actual downstream effects on human decision-making and wellbeing. The framework also supports research into tradeoffs between different values, such as between helpfulness and avoiding value manipulation.
